worth_it_explanation: A Samba TV measurement study that does not reflect the actual campaign, the correct audience, or the full set of screens being bought produces reach and frequency figures that describe the wrong thing. The practitioner and the client may act on those figures, adjusting media spend or reporting lift against a baseline that never matched the buy. Getting the study configuration right before the campaign launches takes a short review session and prevents a reporting problem that is difficult to correct retroactively once the flight window has closed.
intro: Samba TV uses Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data from opt-in smart TVs to measure cross-screen advertising: deduplicated reach, frequency, demographic and household audience figures, incremental reach over linear TV, and outcomes across linear TV, connected TV (CTV), and digital screens. The measurement depends on a correctly configured study that defines the campaign being measured. When the study configuration does not match the actual buy, the measurement output describes a different campaign than the one that ran. These checks examine whether the target audience defined in the study matches the intended audience, whether all channels and screens in the campaign are included in the measurement scope, and whether the campaign definition and flight dates align with the actual delivery window.

audit_checks.title: The target audience defined in the Samba TV study does not match the campaign's intended audience
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the study's target audience is defined differently than the campaign's intended audience, the on-target reach and frequency figures measure delivery against the wrong group. The campaign may appear to be reaching its intended audience efficiently when the measurement is actually counting a different demographic or household segment.

educate.base:
In a Samba TV cross-screen measurement study, the target audience definition determines which demographic or household segment is used to calculate on-target impressions, on-target reach, and on-target frequency. If the study defines the target audience as Adults 25-54 but the campaign was planned and bought against Adults 18-34, the on-target figures in the Samba TV report describe how well the campaign reached a group it was not trying to reach. The practitioner reviewing the report may reach incorrect conclusions about audience efficiency.

Audience definitions in cross-screen measurement studies use age-and-gender breaks, household income tiers, or custom household segments depending on the platform and the study type. Samba TV's ACR (Automatic Content Recognition) data is used to identify TV-viewing households, and the audience segments attached to those households come from third-party data partnerships or from Samba TV's own panel demographics. The practitioner configuring the study needs to confirm that the segment selected in the study tool matches the segment the media plan was built around, not just a default or a convenient approximation.

A few signs suggest the audience definition may not match the campaign's intent:
  • The study's target audience segment uses a different age break or gender split than the campaign's media plan or the insertion order (IO)
  • The study uses a default household income or psychographic segment that was not specified in the campaign brief
  • The on-target reach percentage appears unusually high or low relative to what the buy was designed to deliver
You may want to pull the campaign brief or media plan and compare the intended audience directly against the audience segment shown in the Samba TV study configuration before reviewing the report's on-target figures.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail campaign optimized against a purchaser or in-market household segment, you may want to confirm the Samba TV study's target audience reflects a segment defined around purchase intent or purchase behavior rather than a broad demographic break that may have been used as a proxy.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation campaign targeting a specific demographic tier such as high-income households or small-business decision makers, you may want to confirm the study's audience definition matches the demographic or firmographic segment in the campaign brief rather than a default age-and-gender break.

educate.learn_more_note: Authoritative guidance on Samba TV study audience configuration is in the gated Samba TV measurement documentation. Contact your Samba TV account team for access to the measurement setup guide.

investigate.intro: Pull the study configuration from your Samba TV account and compare the audience definition to the campaign brief or media plan.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Log in to your Samba TV reporting portal and open the measurement study associated with the campaign you are auditing.
Navigate to the study's configuration or setup section and locate the target audience definition. Note the age break, gender split, household income tier, and any custom segment applied.
Pull the campaign brief, media plan, or insertion order (IO) for the same campaign and locate the intended target audience specification.
Compare the two definitions. Note any age break, gender, or segment differences between the study configuration and the campaign brief.
If the study audience differs from the campaign brief, note the discrepancy and flag whether the difference is material enough to affect on-target reach and frequency figures in the report.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the target audience definition from the Samba TV study and the intended audience from the campaign brief. If the two match on age break, gender split, and any custom segment, the check passes. If the study audience differs from the brief on any of those dimensions, the check is a fail, recorded with both definitions and the specific discrepancy.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the study's target audience definition and the campaign brief's intended audience above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the target audience defined in the Samba TV measurement study for {{site_name}}'s campaign does not match the campaign's intended audience as specified in the media plan. The on-target reach and frequency figures in the report measure delivery against a segment that differs from the one the campaign was planned and bought against, making it difficult to evaluate whether the buy reached its intended audience. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the study configuration against the campaign brief and updating the audience definition so future reporting reflects the correct target segment.

audit_checks.title: The measurement study does not include all channels or screens the campaign runs on
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a Samba TV study captures only a subset of the campaign's channels or screens, the cross-screen reach and frequency figures undercount total exposure. Deduplicated reach will be understated, and incremental reach calculations will not reflect the full media mix, which can lead to incorrect conclusions about which channels are delivering unique audiences.

educate.base:
Samba TV cross-screen measurement is designed to provide a unified, deduplicated view of reach and frequency across linear TV, connected TV (CTV), and digital screens. The value of the measurement is specifically its ability to identify households or individuals exposed across multiple screens and eliminate duplicate counts. That deduplication only works when all of the screens where the campaign ran are included in the study's scope.

Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data captured by Samba TV's smart TV panel covers linear and CTV exposure for opted-in households. Digital and social exposure is typically brought in through pixel-based data or publisher-level integrations that must be explicitly configured for the study. A study that captures linear TV and CTV but does not include the digital leg of the campaign will report cross-screen figures that are actually cross-TV figures, not the full cross-screen picture the media plan was built around.

This gap matters when incremental reach is one of the campaign's key performance indicators (KPIs). Incremental reach measures how much additional audience digital or CTV delivery reaches beyond linear TV. If digital is excluded from the study, the incremental reach calculation only compares CTV to linear, and the digital media spend's contribution to audience extension is invisible in the report.

A few signs suggest the measurement scope may not cover the full campaign:
  • The study configuration lists fewer channels or media types than the insertion order or media plan
  • Digital or social line items appear in the media plan but no pixel or integration is configured in the study for those channels
  • The campaign ran across linear TV, CTV, and paid digital, but the Samba TV report shows only a linear-versus-CTV breakdown
You may want to review the study's channel or integration settings alongside the media plan to confirm each line item's screen or channel has a corresponding measurement input in the study.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail campaign running across linear TV, CTV, and digital display or video, you may want to confirm that the Samba TV study includes an integration for the digital display or video placements so the cross-screen reach figure reflects the full investment rather than only the TV portion.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation campaign supplementing TV with paid search or paid social, you may want to confirm whether the study is scoped to cover digital channels or whether it is limited to TV and CTV exposure only, and evaluate whether the missing channels affect how incremental reach is reported.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Cross-Media Audience Measurement Standards, Phase I Video

educate.learn_more_note: Authoritative guidance on Samba TV study channel and screen configuration is in the gated Samba TV measurement documentation. Contact your Samba TV account team for access to the measurement setup guide.

investigate.intro: Review the Samba TV study's channel or integration configuration and compare it to the full list of channels and screens in the media plan or insertion order.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Log in to your Samba TV reporting portal and open the measurement study associated with the campaign.

Navigate to the study's configuration and locate the channel, screen, or integration settings. List every channel or media type included in the measurement scope: linear TV, CTV, digital video, digital display, paid social, or other.
Pull the media plan, insertion order, or campaign brief and list every channel and screen where the campaign ran or was planned to run.
Compare the two lists. Identify any channel or screen present in the media plan that does not have a corresponding input in the study configuration.

For digital channels, confirm whether a pixel or publisher integration was set up and active during the campaign flight. A pixel that was never fired or an integration that was not completed before the campaign launched will create a gap even if the channel appears in the study settings.
Note any missing channel and flag whether its absence affects an incremental reach KPI in the report.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the channels included in the study configuration and the channels in the media plan. If all channels in the media plan have a corresponding measurement input in the study, the check passes. If any channel in the media plan is absent from the study, or if a digital integration was set up but never activated during the flight, the check is a fail, recorded with the missing channel and a note on whether it affects an incremental reach KPI.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the study's channel configuration and the media plan's channel list above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Samba TV measurement study for {{site_name}}'s campaign does not include all of the channels or screens the campaign ran on. One or more channels present in the media plan do not have a corresponding measurement input in the study, so the cross-screen reach and frequency figures undercount total exposure and the incremental reach calculations do not reflect the full media mix. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the study's channel configuration against the media plan and adding the missing channel integrations before the next campaign flight so the cross-screen measurement reflects the full buy.

audit_checks.title: The campaign definition or flight dates in Samba TV do not match the actual buy
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the flight dates or campaign boundaries in the Samba TV study do not match the actual delivery window, the measurement captures exposure outside the campaign period or misses exposure from the actual run. Reach and frequency figures may include unrelated activity from an adjacent campaign, or the report may reflect only part of the flight.

educate.base:
A Samba TV measurement study is built around a specific campaign definition that includes the campaign's name or identifier, the measurement period (start and end dates), and the set of impressions or exposures to track. When the study's flight dates or campaign definition are set up before the actual buy is finalized, they often do not get updated if the flight is extended, shortened, or rescheduled.

A study that starts too early will include pre-flight impressions in its reach and frequency calculations. A study that ends before the campaign does will miss delivery from the final days or weeks of the flight. A study associated with the wrong campaign name or identifier may pull in impressions from a different campaign entirely, particularly in accounts where multiple campaigns run simultaneously under similar names.

For audience measurement studies using ACR data, the measurement window matters because ACR exposure is tied to a specific time period. Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data captures what an opted-in smart TV household watched or was exposed to within the defined window. Impressions outside that window are either not captured or are captured and counted in a period they were not assigned to.

A few signs suggest the flight dates or campaign definition may not match the actual buy:
  • The study's start or end date differs from the campaign's actual delivery start or end date in the ad server or platform
  • The campaign has been extended or modified since the study was first configured, and the study configuration was not updated to match
  • The study's name or identifier references a campaign version, budget period, or flight that does not correspond to the current buy
You may want to pull the actual delivery dates from the ad server or platform and compare them to the study's configured measurement window before relying on the reach and frequency figures in the report.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail campaign tied to a promotional period such as a seasonal sale or product launch, you may want to confirm the Samba TV study's measurement window aligns exactly with the promotional flight rather than a broader planning period that includes pre-launch or post-sale delivery.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation campaign with a fixed budget period or a defined enrollment window, you may want to confirm the study's flight dates match the period during which leads were being generated rather than a broader period that includes non-lead-generating activity.

educate.learn_more_note: Authoritative guidance on Samba TV study flight date and campaign definition configuration is in the gated Samba TV measurement documentation. Contact your Samba TV account team for access to the measurement setup guide.

investigate.intro: Compare the Samba TV study's configured flight dates and campaign definition to the actual delivery dates and campaign identifiers from the ad server or platform.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Log in to your Samba TV reporting portal and open the measurement study associated with the campaign.
Note the study's configured start date, end date, and campaign name or identifier.
Pull the campaign's actual delivery dates from the ad server, demand-side platform, or media plan. Note the actual start date, end date, and any flight modifications made after the campaign launched.

Compare the study's flight dates to the actual delivery dates. Flag any gap between the configured end date and the actual last delivery date, or any overlap between the configured start date and a pre-launch period.

If the campaign was extended or modified after the study was configured, confirm whether the study configuration was updated to match. A study that has not been updated will have a measurement window that does not cover the full flight.
Check the campaign name or identifier in the study against the name used in the ad server or platform. A mismatch may indicate the study was associated with the wrong campaign version.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the study's configured start date and end date alongside the campaign's actual delivery start and end date. If the dates align within a reasonable tolerance and the campaign identifier matches, the check passes. If the study's measurement window begins before actual delivery started, ends before the campaign finished, or is associated with a different campaign name or identifier than the one that ran, the check is a fail, recorded with both date sets and the specific discrepancy.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the study's configured flight dates and the campaign's actual delivery dates above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the campaign definition or flight dates configured in the Samba TV measurement study for {{site_name}} do not align with the campaign's actual delivery window. The study's measurement period either begins before delivery started, ends before the campaign finished, or is associated with a campaign name or identifier that does not match the buy that ran. Reach and frequency figures derived from this study may reflect exposure outside the intended flight or may miss delivery from part of the campaign. {{voice_recommend}} updating the study configuration to align with the actual delivery dates and confirming the campaign identifier matches the buy before using the study's output for reporting or optimization decisions.
